abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming graduate research, writing, and decision-making in higher education, yet student engagement with these tools often remains uncritical, ethically uncertain, and rhetorically passive. This article reconceptualizes graduate AI competence as holistic AI literacy that integrates media, critical, and rhetorical dimensions. Using an exploratory case study design, the study analyzes survey data from graduate students across four national contexts, complemented by observations from public academic events on AI in higher education. Findings reveal a persistent use–competence gap: frequent reliance on AI coexists with limited verification practices, ethical ambiguity, and low awareness of AI’s persuasive influence. In response, the article proposes a holistic AI literacy model and outlines implications for curriculum design, assessment practices, and institutional policies supporting responsible human–AI collaboration in graduate education.
title: Rethinking Graduate AI Competence: A Holistic AI Literacy Model for Higher Education

abstract: <jats:p>Artificial intelligence (AI) is now becoming a pivotal force identified as an important driver in the rapidly transforming higher education landscape, requiring both educators and students to have a thorough understanding of it. This study adopts both scoping and integrative review methodologies to analyze existing literature between 2020 and 2025. The analysis discerns the significant themes, such as integration, governance, policy, ethics, social responsibility convergence of technology, ethics, teaching methods, and social consequences, and literacy. Research indicates a substantial disparity in the incorporation of ethical frameworks and the practical implementation of AI literacy in higher education curriculum (Zhang et al., 2024). The study concludes with the recommendation to bridge the gap, highlighting the necessity for multidisciplinary approaches and policy development by convergence of technology, ethics, teaching methods, and societal impact.</jats:p>
title: AI Literacy in Higher Education
container-title: Advances in Computational Intelligence and Robotics
subtitle: A Scoping and Integrative Review

abstract: This paper presents the development, refinement, and validation of the Critical Artificial Intelligence Literacy Scale, an instrument designed to measure artificial intelligence literacy across four dimensions: knowledge-related, operational, critical, and ethical. The initial version of the questionnaire, based on a robust theoretical framework and expert consultation, included 40 items and was tested with 57 doctoral students. It demonstrated strong psychometric properties (comparative fit index = 0.946, Tucker-Lewis index = 0.92) but showed limitations such as item redundancy (α = 0.947) and low performance of general items. To address these issues, the questionnaire was refined to a concise 24-item version. The revised instrument was evaluated using a sample of 314 first-year student teachers. Exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses confirmed a four-factor structure, with each dimension demonstrating strong reliability (Cronbach's alpha ranging from 0.838 to 0.912) and excellent model fit indices (comparative fit index = 0.960, root mean square error of approximation = 0.0441). The results validate the Critical Artificial Intelligence Literacy Scale as a reliable and efficient tool for assessing artificial intelligence literacy in educational settings.
title: AI Literacy in Higher Education
